title: Iran Declares Strait of Hormuz 'Completely Open' During Lebanon Ceasefire
detail: Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi announced on social media that 'the passage for all commercial vessels through Strait of Hormuz is declared completely open for the remaining period of ceasefire,' tying the opening to the US-brokered 10-day ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon. However, shipping operators reported confusion as ships attempting to transit were turned back by Iranian forces unless their cargoes were cleared by Iranian authorities. Vessels linked to 'hostile nations' remained blocked. Iranian gunboats maintained checkpoints at the strait's chokepoints.

media.caption: Iran declares Strait of Hormuz open for commercial vessels during Lebanon ceasefire

title: US-Brokered 10-Day Israel-Lebanon Ceasefire Takes Effect
detail: A US-brokered 10-day ceasefire between Israel and Lebanon/Hezbollah took effect on April 17, 2026, creating a fragile calm after weeks of Israeli bombardment and ground operations in southern Lebanon. More than one million displaced Lebanese began cautiously returning home, with traffic backed up for kilometers along the route to the Litani River. In Beirut's Haret Hreik neighborhood — struck 62 times over six weeks — residents found 'entire buildings reduced to rubble.' Lebanese President Aoun declared the nation would 'no longer be a pawn' in a broader conflict. Israel reserved the right to self-defense and maintained troop deployments in southern Lebanon.

media.caption: Displaced Lebanese families return home as 10-day ceasefire takes effect

title: Oil Prices Plunge 9–11% and Wall Street Hits Record on Hormuz Opening
detail: Global oil markets reacted dramatically to Iran's declaration that the Strait of Hormuz was open. Brent crude fell 9.07% to settle at $90.38 per barrel, down from a recent peak of ~$120. WTI crude plunged 11.45% to $83.85 per barrel — its lowest since March 10. US stock markets surged: the Dow gained 869 points (+1.79%), recouping all war-related losses. The S&P 500 leaped 1.2% to an all-time record high, closing a third consecutive week of big gains. Analysts cautioned the market euphoria was premature given the conditional nature of the Hormuz opening and unresolved blockade tensions.

media.caption: Oil prices plunge and stock markets soar after Iran reopens Strait of Hormuz

title: Trump Thanks Iran but Declares US Naval Blockade Remains 'In Full Force'
detail: President Trump thanked Tehran for opening the Strait of Hormuz but issued a statement declaring the US naval blockade of Iranian ports would remain fully in effect. Trump posted: 'THE NAVAL BLOCKADE WILL REMAIN IN FULL FORCE AND EFFECT AS IT PERTAINS TO IRAN, ONLY, UNTIL SUCH TIME AS OUR TRANSACTION WITH IRAN IS 100% COMPLETE.' Pentagon data showed the blockade had deterred at least 13 ships from transiting. Trump expressed optimism about reaching a deal with Iran, stating negotiations were going 'very well' with no 'sticking points.'

media.caption: US naval blockade remains in force as Iran opens and then closes Hormuz

title: Iranian Parliament Speaker Ghalibaf: Hormuz Will Not Stay Open While US Blockade Continues
detail: Iranian Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf scrapped the Hormuz reopening within hours of Araghchi's announcement, insisting the strait would remain closed as long as the US naval blockade of Iranian ports continued. Ghalibaf accused the United States of 'piracy and maritime robbery under the so-called blockade' and stated that Iran could not allow the Strait of Hormuz to remain open while the US continued 'acts of piracy.' The contradictory statements from Iran's foreign minister and parliament speaker created significant confusion among shipping operators and diplomats.

media.caption: Iranian officials threaten to close Hormuz as contradiction emerges over opening
